title: Paper Explained: A multimodal and temporal foundation model for virtual patient representations at healthcare system scale - A Beginner's Guide
subtitle:
- A Patient Atlas for Predicting Health Journeys
- Unified View of Patients’ Health Journeys
- Mapping a Patient's Medical Journey with AI
- The Medical Journey Atlas That Predicts Health

content.background:
Before this work, health data lived in many separate places and in many different formats. Labs, imaging, doctors’ notes, medications, procedures, and even administrative records all sat in their own silos, with different codes and layouts. And these records often span decades, but not in a neatly connected way. It’s like trying to read a person’s entire health story from pieces scattered in dozens of notebooks, each written in a slightly different language. This fragmentation makes it hard to see the full patient picture or to learn general patterns about how diseases start and change over time.

AI researchers also faced a big hurdle: most models only learn from one type of data at a time (say, just images or just text) or from short time windows. But a patient’s health is a long, evolving journey where many kinds of information interact over many years. Without a single, coherent view, predicting long-term risk, understanding how a treatment might work for an individual, or spotting potential adverse events becomes guesswork. Clinicians also need explanations that tie predictions back to real, understandable signals in the data, not just black-box numbers. And there’s a practical need to be able to search and reason across diverse data types and patients, not just run isolated analyses.

This motivates the research: to create a unified, scalable representation of a patient that can harmonize all these data types into one “atlas” of medical concepts. By building a model that can capture the full span of a patient’s care—from multiple modalities to long histories—it becomes possible to forecast future health events years ahead, track disease progression, anticipate treatment responses, and even improve hospital operations. In short, the goal is to transform sprawling, siloed medical records into computable, actionable knowledge that supports proactive, personalized care.
content.methodology:
Apollo is a big idea: instead of treating different kinds of medical data as separate, it tries to build one unified “language” and a timeline that describe every patient’s full care journey. They pulled together decades of hospital records from millions of patients, covering many data types like lab results, imaging, clinical notes, medications, procedures, and more. The key innovation is to create a single, shared representation space that can hold all these multimodal signals and remember how they unfold over time. In other words, Apollo turns a scattered, messy history into a single, compact virtual patient that embodies who the patient is and what happened to them across many years.

How they did it, conceptually, in a few simple steps:
- Gather and harmonize a massive, longitudinal source of data from a large hospital system, spanning many data types (the 28 modalities) and 30+ years.
- Build an “atlas of medical concepts”—a common vocabulary of about 100,000 medical events that covers the different data types and specialties.
- Train a model to map these diverse events and their timing into a unified, temporal representation—essentially compressing a long sequence of structured and unstructured notes into a single virtual patient embedding that still preserves the evolution of care.
- Test this representation on hundreds of tasks to see if it can forecast health trajectories, reason about treatments, and help with retrieval and search, all while linking predictions back to intelligible clinical signals.

What Apollo can do and why it matters (the WHAT and HOW, in plain terms):
- Prognosis and outcome forecasting: the model can predict things like new disease onset years in advance, disease progression, and how a patient might respond to treatments.
- Risk and safety assessments: it can estimate the chance of treatment-related adverse events and other hospital-relevant endpoints.
- Multimodal reasoning and retrieval: beyond predictions, the embeddings enable semantic similarity searches and retrieval tasks, effectively letting clinicians find patients or cases that look similar in the full multimodal history.
- Text and image as fuel for a medical search engine: Apollo supports queries that mix written notes with imaging information, offering a multimodal search experience over the entire patient record.

In short, Apollo showcases a practical path toward computable medicine: a single, temporally aware, multimodal patient representation that can be used to forecast outcomes, answer clinical questions, and search across the full breadth of a patient’s medical journey. The approach emphasizes learning from the whole context, interpreting predictions with clinically meaningful signals, and enabling tools that reason over the entire care timeline rather than isolated data points.
content.results:
Apollo is a big step toward turning every piece of a patient’s medical story into one usable, big-picture understanding. It combines many kinds of data—lab results, notes, images, scans, medications, and more—from decades of hospital records across millions of patients, into a single, unified representation called the “atlas of medical concepts.” Think of it like turning the entire patient journey into a compact, multidimensional fingerprint that captures what happened, when, and why, across many doctors and departments. This is powered by a multimodal temporal foundation model, meaning it learns from both numbers and text, and from pictures as well, while keeping track of how things evolve over time.

What Apollo can do goes well beyond previous systems. It can forecast a wide range of future health events long before they happen—up to several years ahead in some cases—and it can also answer retrieval questions, like finding patients who resemble a given case or returning relevant information when you search with text or an image. In practical terms, this means better early warning of diseases, smarter predictions about how diseases will progress, and clearer expectations about which treatments will work or cause side effects. Importantly, the model’s explanations line up with real, clinically meaningful signs people already look for in practice, so doctors can trust the AI’s guidance.

The significance is that Apollo provides a foundation for computable medicine at system scale. By unifying diverse data sources and many medical specialties into one model, it helps remove silos that split health information and enables reasoning over an entire patient’s care journey. The practical impact could include earlier and more personalized interventions, safer treatment choices, and more efficient hospital operations. In short, Apollo moves us toward a future where comprehensive, data-rich patient portraits guide decisions across the whole health system.
content.significance:
This paper matters today because it tackles a fundamental problem in healthcare AI: data are everywhere but rarely in one place. Apollo builds a single, multimodal, temporally aware representation of a patient by learning from decades of hospital records, images, and clinical notes. This “virtual patient” or atlas of medical concepts lets the model summarize a full care journey, not just a single test or snapshot. The result is a system that can forecast long-term outcomes, predict disease onset years in advance, assess treatment responses, and even support hospital operations—all from a unified embedding. That shift from isolated predictions to holistic, longitudinal reasoning is exactly what we need to handle real-world medicine at scale.

In the long run, Apollo helped push the AI community toward true foundation-style models for healthcare. Its emphasis on aligning predictions with interpretable, multimodal biomarkers and on cross-modal retrieval foreshadowed later work on multimodal medical copilots, prognosis dashboards, and decision-support tools that draw on both structured data and free-text notes or images. The work also aligns with the broader trend of retrieval-augmented AI, where models pull relevant evidence from large medical corpora to justify decisions, a pattern now common in modern AI systems. While this paper comes from a single health system, its ideas shaped how researchers and vendors think about building reusable, scalable representations of patients that can power a wide range of tasks—from risk prediction to personalized treatment planning.

Connecting to tools people know today, Apollo sits alongside the same family of ideas that underlie ChatGPT-style copilots and multimodal assistants. Modern AI systems increasingly combine language, images, and structured data, and they use retrieval to ground answers in real patient information. In healthcare, this translates into clinical copilots that can ingest EHRs, imaging, and notes to help clinicians with prognosis, diagnostics, and care optimization. Apollo’s “unified patient representation” concept provides a roadmap for these systems: a single, rich embedding that enables flexible querying, robust forecasting, and explainable reasoning, making computable medicine more practical and safer to deploy at scale.

concept_explanation.title: Understanding Virtual patient representations: The Heart of A multimodal and temporal foundation model for virtual patient representations at healthcare system scale
concept_explanation.content:
Think of a patient as a long, winding journey through care: tests, symptoms, doctors’ notes, images, medications, procedures, and more—spanning years. A virtual patient representation is like a single, compact fingerprint or map of that entire journey. It compresses all the important details from many kinds of data (lab results, diagnoses, radiology images, text notes, etc.) into one fixed-length numeric vector. In the paper, this compact summary is built to capture not just what happened, but how things evolved over time, so you can reason about the patient even when you’re looking at a different system or a new task.

What makes this representation possible is a huge, unified vocabulary of medical concepts and a model that learns from many kinds of data all at once. The researchers collected 25 billion records from 7.2 million patients, including 28 data modalities like structured codes, lab values, imaging, and clinical notes. They build an “atlas of medical concepts” with about 100 thousand distinct events, and they train a model to understand how these events relate to one another across time. The result is a shared representation space where each patient’s history is condensed into a virtual patient vector, which can be compared to other patients, searched for similar cases, or fed into predictive tools.

Here’s how it works, step by step, in simple terms:
1) Gather longitudinal data from many sources: every visit, test result, image, worded note, medication, and procedure across years. 2) Create a common vocabulary of events and concepts (for example, a diagnosis code paired with a lab trend or an imaging finding) so everything can be talked about in a unified way. 3) Train a large multimodal model on sequences of these events from many patients, so the model learns patterns about how health evolves over time. 4) From this trained model, extract a fixed-length virtual patient representation for each patient (or at a given point in time). 5) Use these embeddings for downstream tasks, such as predicting who will develop a disease, how a disease will progress, how a treatment might work for an individual, and even hospital-level outcomes. 6) Evaluate the approach on lots of held-out tasks to show it generalizes, and use attribution methods to see which parts of the history most influenced a prediction.

A concrete example helps. Imagine predicting the risk of heart failure years in the future for a patient with diabetes. The virtual representation would encode long-term patterns like A1c trends, blood pressure, kidney function tests, retinopathy imaging notes, medications started or stopped, and prior hospital visits. The model can then compare this patient’s representation to others to estimate future risk, or be used to identify similar patients who followed different care paths, to learn which interventions worked best. The same embeddings can power different tasks—forecasting disease onset up to five years ahead, tracking how a disease is likely to progress, predicting response to therapies, flagging potential adverse events, or helping hospital systems forecast admissions and resource needs. The researchers even show that you can perform semantic similarity searches—finding patients with clinically similar journeys using text or image queries.

Why is this important? Because it offers a computable, unified view of a person’s entire medical history, cutting through data silos that traditionally separate labs, imaging, notes, and prescriptions. A virtual patient representation provides a single, machine-understandable summary that enables reliable reasoning and comparison across time and modalities. For clinicians, that means tools that can forecast risk earlier, suggest personalized treatment options, and surface relevant prior experiences from millions of patients. For researchers and hospitals, it enables scalable retrieval and analysis over huge patient populations, supporting both personalized medicine and system-wide improvements.

Practical applications include prognosis and risk forecasting (e.g., predicting who will develop a disease, who will respond to a therapy, or who is at risk of an adverse event), disease trajectory modeling, and treatment optimization. The approach also acts like a multimodal search engine: you can query with text or even images to find similar patient journeys or case reports across the data lake. Of course, there are caveats to keep in mind: the model learns from data within a single health system, so generalizability to other hospitals needs careful validation, and privacy-plus-bias concerns require strong safeguards and ongoing monitoring. But if done carefully, virtual patient representations can become a powerful backbone for computable medicine—helping clinicians make sense of complex histories and enabling AI-assisted care at scale.

summary: This paper introduces Apollo, a multimodal temporal foundation model that builds a unified, scalable representation of whole patient care from decades of hospital data, becoming the foundation for prognostic forecasting, multimodal retrieval, and computable medicine across the healthcare system.
